writingStyle.description: Language quality guidance injected into all LLM prompts
writingStyle.titles: 3-6 words. Plain noun phrase that names the specific error. No parentheticals or clarifications inside parentheses. Write the error directly: 'Did Not Flip the Second Fraction' — never 'Did Not Flip the Second Fraction (no reciprocal)'.
writingStyle.descriptions: 2-3 sentences maximum. Keep it concrete and specific. Do NOT start every sentence with 'Students' — vary the subject. Good openers: the error itself ('Slope and intercept get swapped...'), a conditional ('When reading y = mx + b, the two numbers...'), or the consequence ('The result is...'). One idea per sentence. No hedging words (often, typically, usually, tend to). Do not restate the same idea in different words. Write for a classroom teacher, not a researcher.
writingStyle.instructionalMoves: Write in short, scannable chunks. Use plain conversational language — imagine a teacher reading this quickly between classes. Break steps into separate sentences. Avoid dense paragraphs. No academic or clinical language. Active voice. Each instruction should be one action: 'Show students two worked examples side by side.' not 'Teachers should consider presenting students with two worked examples simultaneously for comparison purposes.'
writingStyle.successIndicators: Start each with an action verb. Describe observable student behavior only. 10 words max per indicator.
writingStyle.overviewBullets: Each bullet starts with a short bolded action verb phrase (e.g. 'Think', 'Discuss', 'Present'). Follow with 1-2 sentences max. Write for a teacher skimming between classes.

nextStepOption.description: microcoachNextStepOption — generates one classroom activity per format per misconception
designPrinciples: Misconception-driven: The activity must directly address the identified cognitive error — not provide generic practice on the standard.
Error-first instruction: Students must encounter and analyze incorrect reasoning before seeing the correct method.

Connection to student responses: The activity must reference or build on the specific error patterns observed in the class data (e.g. the core misconception, or a 'Favorite No' the teacher could highlight).

activityStructures.description: Each activity must have a clearly named structure. The following are examples of strong structures — use them or invent other distinct structures that fit the misconception and format. The structure must be meaningfully different from any other activity generated for the same misconception.

structures.name: Incorrect Worked Example Analysis
structures.description: Students examine a complete incorrect solution step-by-step, identify exactly where the reasoning goes wrong, and justify a correction.

structures.name: Compare / Debate / Evaluate Strategies
structures.description: Students evaluate two or more solution methods to determine which is correct or more efficient. May include structured debate or discussion to justify reasoning.

structures.name: Favorite No
structures.description: Analyze a real or hypothetical student error (anonymous): identify what the student understood, name the misconception, and explain the correct reasoning.

structures.name: Math Hospital / Diagnose & Repair
structures.description: Students 'treat' an incorrect solution: identify the error, propose a correction, and explain why it works. Can be framed as a cartoon character's incorrect solution to highlight reasoning errors.

structures.name: Predict Misconceptions / Error Forecasting
structures.description: Students anticipate where errors are likely to occur in a problem before solving, then discuss strategies to prevent them.

structures.name: Strategy Construction / Corrective Pathway
structures.description: After analyzing errors, students generate a correct solution pathway from scratch, articulating each step.

structures.name: Multiple Entry Points / Representations
structures.description: Students engage with the misconception through visual, symbolic, or verbal representations — e.g. graph, number line, table, equation — to surface where the error appears in each form.

distinctnessRules: Each activity generated for a misconception must use a different activityStructure.
Activities must differ meaningfully in at least one of: instructional approach, activity structure, strategy tag, or representation/explanation strategy.
Do not rephrase the same activity with minor wording changes (e.g. 'Clear Denominators First' vs 'Clear the Denominators First' is not distinct).
Do not generate the same structure in two different formats (e.g. Incorrect Worked Example Analysis | Whole Class and Incorrect Worked Example Analysis | Split Class is not distinct enough).
classroomFeasibility: Require minimal setup — activity should be implementable within 30–60 seconds of classroom transition.
Use simple, familiar instructional routines that early-career teachers can execute without extensive prep.
Avoid complex classroom logistics: no multiple rounds of regrouping, no complicated grouping structures, no detailed classroom management procedures.
Focus on instructional moves rather than logistics — describe what the teacher says and does, not how to move furniture.

formatConstraints.description: Only two formats are allowed. Do not generate Small Groups.

wholeClass.label: whole_class
wholeClass.description: All students engage in the same activity led by the teacher. Common structures: analyzing incorrect worked examples, teacher modeling and student critique, whole-class discussion, quick formative responses (whiteboards, hand signals, polling). May briefly include turn-and-talk or think-pair-share, but the activity must remain primarily teacher-led whole-class instruction.

splitClass.label: split_class
splitClass.description: The class is divided into exactly TWO groups based on a response pattern or misconception — e.g. students who chose answers A/B vs. C/D, or students who showed misconception X vs. misconception Y.
structures: Teacher Rotation: Teacher alternates between the two groups providing targeted support.
Parallel Error Analysis: Each group analyzes a different incorrect example, then the class reconvenes to compare reasoning.
Partner Discussion Within Groups: Students may think-pair-share within their half of the class.

udlRequirements: Visual representations: specify exactly what the teacher should draw or write on the board.
Discussion prompts: include 2–3 example questions the teacher can ask to surface student reasoning.
Comparison of strategies: include 2–3 solution strategies or reasoning approaches students can compare.
Incorrect solution analysis: activities should include 2–3 incorrect worked examples that illustrate the misconception.
incorrectWorkedExampleRules: Each example contains exactly ONE intentional error: the specific misconception step. All other arithmetic and setup must be mathematically correct.
Trace the incorrect reasoning honestly: after the misconception step, continue computing correctly from that wrong intermediate value.
Never introduce arithmetic slippage (wrong multiplication, wrong simplification, wrong sign) in steps that are not the misconception itself.

Self-check each example: imagine a student who understands all the math but makes only this one specific conceptual error. Would they produce exactly this work? If not, fix the surrounding arithmetic.

The error MUST produce a wrong final answer. After you trace the incorrect path, verify that the final answer differs from the correct answer. If the problem you chose happens to produce the correct answer despite the misconception error, choose a different problem where the same conceptual error leads to a clearly wrong final answer.

incorrectWorkedExampleFewShot.misconception: Student multiplies fractions without flipping the divisor (does not apply keep-change-flip)

good.label: CORRECT — only the misconception step has an error; all arithmetic from that point is correct

bad.label: INCORRECT — introduces unintentional arithmetic error outside the misconception step
bad.incorrectWork:
3/4 ÷ 2/5
= 3/4 × 2/5  ← does not flip
= 6/20
= 2/5  ✗ wrong simplification; 6/20 = 3/10, not 2/5

incorrectWorkedExampleFewShot.misconception: Student swaps slope and y-intercept when reading y = mx + b

good.label: CORRECT — only the swap step is wrong; everything else follows correctly from it
good.problem: Find the slope and y-intercept of y = 3x + 5.
good.incorrectWork:
y = 3x + 5
Slope = 5, y-intercept = 3  ← swaps m and b
The line rises 5 for every 1 right, and crosses the y-axis at (0, 3).

bad.label: INCORRECT — adds a second unrelated error after the swap
bad.problem: Find the slope and y-intercept of y = 3x + 5.
bad.incorrectWork:
y = 3x + 5
Slope = 5, y-intercept = 3  ← swaps m and b
The line rises 5 for every 2 right  ✗ wrong — slope of 5 means 5 per 1 unit, not per 2

strategyTags.name: Make Structure Visible
strategyTags.whenToUse: Structural conceptual misunderstanding — expose the underlying math structure explicitly

strategyTags.name: Guided Error Analysis
strategyTags.whenToUse: Rule confusion — examine and correct a worked example containing the target error

strategyTags.name: Contrast Correct vs Incorrect
strategyTags.whenToUse: Overgeneralization — compare a correct and incorrect example side-by-side

strategyTags.name: Use Concrete Representation
strategyTags.whenToUse: Conceptual gap — use manipulatives or visual models to ground abstract reasoning

strategyTags.name: Targeted Practice
strategyTags.whenToUse: Partial procedural fluency — focused repetition on the specific error pattern

strategyTags.name: Peer Explanation
strategyTags.whenToUse: Solidifying understanding — students articulate reasoning to each other

strategyTags.name: Whole-Class Reset
strategyTags.whenToUse: Whole-class misunderstanding — reteach from a different angle for the full class

planner.description: Cheap planning call that assigns diverse activity structures across all misconceptions before parallel generation begins

validator.systemPrompt: You are a math accuracy reviewer. Output only valid JSON.
